headline: SOSMA Renewal Cycles: Section 4(11) Requires 5-Year Parliamentary Review of Section 4(5) Detention
context: SOSMA Section 4(11) requires Parliament to extend or let lapse the 28-day pre-charge detention authority in Section 4(5) every five years. The July 2022 renewal passed 111-88. Civil society has called for substantive reform alongside renewal.

cards.big: SOSMA Section 4(11) requires Parliament to extend or let lapse the 28-day pre-charge detention authority every five years.
cards.sub: The July 2022 Dewan Rakyat renewal passed 111 in favour, 88 against, 21 absent. Available renewal records show shorter debates than the 2012 passage.

cards.big: SOSMA Section 4(11) sets the five-year review trigger for the Section 4(5) Superintendent-or-above detention power.
cards.sub: The 2022 renewal was a motion put to a vote. Debate-time allocation is set by the Speaker and government business managers in practice.

cards.big: Suaram reports 6,892 SOSMA arrests since 2012, with 20-28% released without any charge filed.
cards.sub: Most of that operational data emerged after 2012 and is not embedded in the formal renewal debate, though civil society has cited it in submissions.

cards.big: Civil-society organisations (Bar Council, Suaram, Lawyers for Liberty) have made public submissions at renewal moments.
cards.sub: Renewal procedure does not require parliamentary acknowledgement of public submissions, and those submissions are not part of the formal debate record.

cards.big: The renewal mechanism extends an investigative-detention authority. It is not designed to revisit whether the authority should still exist.
Like renewing a licence with a tick-box form: the paperwork advances, the underlying decision is never re-examined.
cards.sub: Renewal procedure is not the same as substantive review.

cards.big: A law permitting 28-day pre-charge detention warrants substantive review at every renewal, anchored on Suaram's arrest and release-without-charge data.
